metadata.description: Optimized scenario definitions for LLM-based classification of financial news
optimization_goals: Clear discriminant signals for pattern matching
Explicit exclusion criteria for boundary cases
Contrastive examples for confusing boundary scenarios

categories.categoryDescription: Regulatory enforcement actions and fines for violations of financial regulations, systematic mistreatment of customers or markets (mis-selling, overcharging, market manipulation, AML failures), corruption, tax evasion facilitation, and ESG misrepresentation. Focus: Bank's business practices harming clients, markets, or regulatory compliance, typically resulting in regulatory fines, customer compensation programs, or class action settlements. Distinguisher: If the primary issue is a regulatory violation or systematic customer harm (even if employees involved), classify as CONDUCT. If an employee acts alone for personal gain without triggering regulatory action, classify as INTERNAL_FRAUD.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Financial institution prosecuted for bribery, corruption, or illegal kickback schemes under FCPA, UKBA, RESPA, or similar laws
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank fined $2bn for bribing foreign officials to secure contracts, or $25m for kickback scheme with title company to steer business
exclusions: If only money laundering without bribery -> 002_FNC
If market manipulation or price fixing without bribes/kickbacks -> 001_ATC

scenarios.shortDefinition: Fines or settlements for antitrust violations, market manipulation, price fixing, or collusion
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank fined $500m for colluding with competitors to fix foreign exchange rates
exclusions: If only insider trading by employee -> 031_URT

scenarios.shortDefinition: Fines or settlements for greenwashing in bank's own ESG disclosures or commitments
keySignals: activist lawsuit on bank's ESG practices
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank fined $100m for overstating its carbon neutrality achievements in annual reports
exclusions: If shareholder lawsuit after ESG scandal -> 043_ESA

scenarios.scenarioName: Breach of AML, Terrorism Financing or Sanctions Regulations
scenarios.shortDefinition: Violations of anti-money laundering, counter-terrorism financing, or sanctions compliance requirements
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank fined $1.9bn for failing to prevent money laundering by drug cartels through inadequate controls
exclusions: If customer tax evasion is primary issue -> 007_CTE

scenarios.scenarioName: Breach of Client Asset Segregation Rules
scenarios.shortDefinition: Fines for failing to properly segregate or protect client money and custody assets
scenarios.typicalExample: Broker fined $50m for failing to segregate $500m of client funds from firm's own accounts
exclusions: If fraud or theft of client assets -> 029_WTX or 030_ICC

scenarios.shortDefinition: Excessive fees or unfair pricing for services that were properly authorized and disclosed, resulting in customer refunds
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank refunds $300m to customers for charging excessive overdraft fees disclosed in terms but unfairly high
exclusions: If product was mis-sold (wrong product for client) -> 010_MSR or 011_MSW
If charged for services without customer consent or enrollment -> 010_MSR

scenarios.shortDefinition: Liability from corporate client bankruptcy due to bank's involvement in client's financial misrepresentation
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank pays $2bn settlement to Enron shareholders for helping company hide debt and mislead investors
exclusions: If pure credit loss from default -> Credit Risk (not operational)

scenarios.shortDefinition: Bank liability for financing projects causing environmental damage or for client's ESG-related default
keySignals: bank liability for client ESG scandal
client default due to ESG dispute
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank pays $200m settlement for financing pipeline project causing major oil spill
exclusions: If bank's own ESG disclosure issue -> 041_BSM

scenarios.shortDefinition: Fines for helping customers evade taxes through undeclared accounts or fraudulent structures
scenarios.typicalExample: Swiss bank pays $2.6bn for helping US clients hide assets and evade taxes
exclusions: If money laundering is primary issue -> 002_FNC

scenarios.shortDefinition: Shareholder lawsuit against bank due to share price drop following ESG misconduct revelation
keySignals: stock price drop after ESG scandal
securities fraud claim related to ESG
scenarios.typicalExample: Shareholders sue bank for $500m after stock drops 30% following fossil fuel financing scandal
exclusions: If regulatory fine for ESG disclosure -> 041_BSM

scenarios.shortDefinition: Omission or misrepresentation of material facts to fund investors, resulting in class action or settlement
scenarios.typicalExample: Asset manager pays $150m to investors for failing to disclose liquidity risks in bond fund

scenarios.shortDefinition: Fund allocation not aligned with ESG objectives, resulting in investor class action
keySignals: green fund investing in fossil fuels
scenarios.typicalExample: Green fund sued for $80m after investors discover 40% holdings in oil and gas companies

scenarios.shortDefinition: Systematic mishandling of loan servicing, foreclosure abuse, or borrower mistreatment during the life of an active loan
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank pays $10bn for foreclosure abuses and improper servicing during 2008 financial crisis
exclusions: If lending process error during origination -> 023_LPE
If selling bad debt or improper debt collection (not active loan servicing) -> 010_MSR

scenarios.shortDefinition: Misrepresentation, deceptive marketing, or sale of unsuitable retail products (loans, credit cards, insurance, add-ons) to many customers, including enrollment without consent
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank pays £40bn in PPI compensation for selling payment protection insurance to customers who didn't need it, or charging for services without proper consent
exclusions: If only excessive pricing of properly disclosed services -> 004_OVC

scenarios.shortDefinition: Misrepresentation or sale of unsuitable investment/corporate banking products to wholesale clients
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank pays $5bn for mis-selling mortgage-backed securities to institutional investors pre-2008

categories.categoryDescription: Malicious cyber attacks by external threat actors (hackers, hacktivist groups, nation-states) targeting THE BANK'S OWN infrastructure, customer data, or funds. Includes data breaches of the bank's systems through hacking, DDoS attacks on the bank, ransomware on the bank, malware in the bank's network, SWIFT network compromise of the bank, and tampering with the bank's databases. CRITICAL REQUIREMENTS: (1) MUST involve intentional attack by external adversary using technical exploits, AND (2) THE BANK ITSELF must be the direct target of the attack. IMPORTANT EXCLUSION: When a THIRD PARTY (merchant, retailer, payment processor) is hacked and card data is stolen, this is NOT a Cyber Risk for the card-issuing bank — it is EXTERNAL_FRAUD (028_MCC), because the bank was not attacked; it merely bears financial losses as the card issuer responsible for reimbursing cardholders. Even if the article describes hacking techniques in detail, if the entity hacked is a merchant/retailer/processor and the bank's role is as card issuer bearing reissuance and fraud costs, classify as EXTERNAL_FRAUD. Distinguisher: If no intentional cyber attack (e.g., accidental data exposure, IT system failure, employee error), classify as DISRUPTION or ERROR instead. If employee intentionally steals data or funds without external hacking, classify as INTERNAL_FRAUD.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Large-scale theft of customer data (>100k records) through a cyber attack directly targeting THE BANK'S OWN systems, excluding card data
keySignals: identity theft risk from bank breach
scenarios.typicalExample: JPMorgan reports that hackers breached ITS OWN systems, exposing personal data of 76 million households. The bank itself was the target of the attack → CYBER (012_CDC).

contrastiveExamples.example: Target's payment systems are hacked, exposing 40 million card numbers. Banks bear costs as card issuers → EXTERNAL_FRAUD because the bank was NOT attacked.
contrastiveExamples.distinction: In 012_CDC, the BANK is the direct target. In 028_MCC, a THIRD PARTY is hacked and the bank pays as card issuer.

exclusions: If card data from a THIRD-PARTY merchant/processor → 028_MCC (EXTERNAL_FRAUD, not CYBER)
If no attack (accidental exposure or IT failure) → DISRUPTION, not cyber
If internal theft by employee without external hacking → 030_ICC (INTERNAL_FRAUD)
attackVectors: Employee credential compromise at the bank
Third-party vendor compromise giving access to bank systems

scenarios.shortDefinition: DDoS or sabotage attack causing unavailability of critical banking services
keySignals: service outage due to cyber attack
scenarios.typicalExample: Major bank's online services unavailable for 3 days due to DDoS attack by hacktivist group
exclusions: If data alteration instead of service disruption -> 015_CDA

scenarios.shortDefinition: Malicious alteration of critical bank data (balances, transactions) through cyber attack
scenarios.typicalExample: Hackers alter customer balances in database, requiring $200m to restore and compensate

scenarios.shortDefinition: Direct theft of funds through cyber attack (ATM hacking, fraudulent transfers, account takeover)
scenarios.typicalExample: Hackers steal $81m through fraudulent SWIFT transfers after infiltrating bank network
exclusions: If external fraud with employee involvement but no cyber -> 029_WTX
If data theft without fund loss -> 012_CDC

categories.categoryDescription: Business continuity disruptions from non-malicious causes: unplanned IT outages, system failures, software bugs, datacenter downtime, natural disasters (earthquakes, hurricanes, floods), pandemics, building destruction (fire, explosion), external infrastructure failures (power grid, SWIFT outage), and critical supplier bankruptcies. Focus: Service unavailability or operational disruption without intentional attack. Distinguisher: If caused by intentional cyber attack, classify as CYBER. If caused by human operational error, classify as ERROR. If intentional sabotage by employee, classify as INTERNAL_FRAUD.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Physical destruction of critical bank building from terrorism, fire, or industrial accident
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank headquarters destroyed by fire, requiring relocation and causing $500m in losses
exclusions: If regional disaster affecting multiple sites -> 020_NDR

scenarios.shortDefinition: IT outage of critical banking application due to software/hardware failure (non-cyber)
scenarios.typicalExample: Core banking system down for 8 hours due to software bug, affecting 10 million customers

scenarios.shortDefinition: Complete datacenter outage affecting multiple critical systems
scenarios.typicalExample: Primary datacenter offline for 12 hours due to power failure, triggering disaster recovery

scenarios.shortDefinition: Unavailability of external payment settlement system (SWIFT, ACH, clearing house)
scenarios.typicalExample: SWIFT network down for 6 hours, bank compensates corporate clients for delayed payments

scenarios.shortDefinition: Major natural disaster (earthquake, hurricane, flood) affecting significant portion of bank operations
scenarios.typicalExample: Hurricane destroys 200 branches and displaces 5,000 employees, causing $800m in losses

scenarios.shortDefinition: Pandemic impact on bank workforce and operations (excluding broader economic effects)
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank incurs $300m in extra costs for remote work infrastructure and extended sick leave during pandemic
exclusions: If broader economic credit losses -> Credit Risk (not operational)
If supplier failure due to pandemic -> 021_SPF
scenarios.note: Excludes pandemic stress on credit/market risk, focuses only on operational impact

scenarios.shortDefinition: Failure or bankruptcy of critical third-party supplier disrupting bank operations
scenarios.typicalExample: Key IT vendor goes bankrupt mid-contract, bank pays $150m to find replacement and migrate systems

categories.categoryDescription: Unintentional human errors and process failures in executing transactions, calculations, or operational procedures: trading errors (fat finger trades), payment mistakes (wrong beneficiary, incorrect amount), failed corporate actions, lending documentation errors (collateral not secured), pricing model bugs, risk model calculation errors (RWA miscalculation), and trading algorithm malfunctions. Focus: Honest mistakes causing financial loss, not misconduct. CRITICAL PRINCIPLE — ROOT CAUSE OVER CONSEQUENCE: Always classify by the ROOT CAUSE of the loss, not by its downstream consequences. Even if an unintentional error ultimately leads to a REGULATORY FINE, ENFORCEMENT ACTION, or RESTATEMENT, the scenario remains ERROR if the root cause was an honest mistake (miscalculation, process failure, model bug). A regulatory fine for a miscalculated RWA is still 037_RME (Error), not CONDUCT. A trading algorithm malfunction that triggers a regulatory sanction is still 038_TAE (Error), not CONDUCT. Only classify as CONDUCT if the act itself was intentional misconduct (deliberate manipulation, systematic customer abuse, willful regulatory violation). Distinguisher: If error is intentional or involves fraud, classify as INTERNAL_FRAUD or CONDUCT. If caused by system failure rather than human/process error, classify as DISRUPTION.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Failure to execute corporate action (IPO, rights issue, dividend) as instructed by client
scenarios.typicalExample: Custodian bank fails to exercise client's rights in $2bn rights issue, liable for $50m lost value

scenarios.shortDefinition: Operational failures in loan origination or servicing causing credit losses
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank loses $200m on defaulted loans due to failure to properly secure collateral during origination
exclusions: If pure credit judgment error -> Credit Risk (not operational)
scenarios.note: Credit loss due to operational error, allocated to operational risk

scenarios.shortDefinition: Error in derivative valuation model causing mispricing and trading losses
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank loses $150m due to coding error in exotic derivatives pricing model over 18 months

scenarios.shortDefinition: Unintentional error in risk-weighted assets (RWA) calculation, capital adequacy reporting, or regulatory model outputs, leading to misstatement of regulatory capital, enforcement actions, or need to raise additional capital. CRITICAL: Even if a regulatory fine or enforcement action results from the error, this remains an ERROR scenario if the root cause was an unintentional miscalculation or process failure — NOT CONDUCT.
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank fined $50m by ECB after discovering its RWA was understated by 20% due to a model calculation error. The bank must raise $5bn in additional capital. Even though a fine was imposed, the root cause is an unintentional model error, so this is ERROR (037_RME), not CONDUCT.

contrastiveExamples.example: Bank deliberately manipulates LIBOR submissions to improve its trading positions → CONDUCT because the act itself is intentional misconduct.
contrastiveExamples.distinction: In 037_RME, the miscalculation is UNINTENTIONAL (bug, oversight, methodology flaw). In CONDUCT, the misrepresentation is DELIBERATE.

contrastiveExamples.example: Bank helps Enron hide debt through deliberate off-balance-sheet structures → CONDUCT because the bank knowingly participated in misrepresentation.
contrastiveExamples.distinction: 037_RME is about the bank's own unintentional errors in its own risk models/reporting, not helping clients commit fraud.

exclusions: If DELIBERATE misstatement to deceive regulators/investors -> 006_CMR or CONDUCT

IMPORTANT: A regulatory fine DOES NOT automatically make it CONDUCT. If the underlying act was an honest calculation/methodological error that happened to trigger a regulatory response, it remains 037_RME (ERROR)
scenarios.classificationPrinciple: Ask: Was the misstatement INTENTIONAL (deliberate manipulation)? → CONDUCT. Was it UNINTENTIONAL (calculation error, methodology flaw, process failure)? → ERROR (037_RME). The presence of a regulatory fine or enforcement action does NOT change this — fines can result from both intentional misconduct AND unintentional errors.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Algorithmic trading malfunction creating unintended market exposure and losses. Includes cases where the malfunctioning algorithm causes market disruption and triggers regulatory fines — the root cause is still an unintentional algorithm error, so this remains ERROR, not CONDUCT.
scenarios.typicalExample: Trading algorithm malfunctions, accumulating $7bn unintended position and losing $440m in 45 minutes. Regulator fines the firm $5m for market disruption caused by the runaway algorithm. Despite the fine, this is ERROR because the algorithm malfunction was unintentional.

contrastiveExamples.example: Bank deliberately programs trading algorithms to spoof the market (place and quickly cancel large orders to manipulate prices) → CONDUCT because the algorithm behavior was INTENTIONALLY designed to manipulate.
contrastiveExamples.distinction: In 038_TAE, the algorithm MALFUNCTIONS (unintended behavior). In CONDUCT, the algorithm works AS DESIGNED but that design is manipulative.

exclusions: If rogue trader intentionally hiding positions -> 031_URT
If algorithm was DELIBERATELY designed to manipulate markets (spoofing by design) -> 001_ATC (CONDUCT)
IMPORTANT: A regulatory fine for market disruption caused by algorithm malfunction does NOT make it CONDUCT. If the algorithm error was unintentional, it remains 038_TAE (ERROR)
scenarios.classificationPrinciple: Ask: Did the algorithm MALFUNCTION (unintended behavior)? → ERROR (038_TAE). Was the algorithm DELIBERATELY designed to manipulate? → CONDUCT (001_ATC).

scenarios.shortDefinition: Manual error in executing or capturing a single market trade
scenarios.typicalExample: Trader accidentally sells $600m instead of $6m, loses $50m unwinding position

scenarios.shortDefinition: Human error on high-value payment transfer (wrong amount, wrong beneficiary)
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank accidentally wires $900m instead of $9m, takes 3 months to recover funds
exclusions: If systematic error -> 027_WTE shouldn't be systematic
If client error (not bank) -> not operational loss

categories.categoryDescription: Fraud schemes orchestrated by external criminals targeting bank or customer funds through social engineering, deception, or exploitation of third-party vulnerabilities: merchant/processor card data breaches, business email compromise (CEO fraud), payment fraud through social engineering, check fraud, account takeover (without hacking). IMPORTANT — MERCHANT/PROCESSOR BREACHES (028_MCC): When a third-party retailer, merchant, or payment processor is hacked and card data is stolen (e.g., Target, Home Depot, Heartland Payment Systems breaches), this is EXTERNAL_FRAUD for the card-issuing bank — NOT Cyber Risk — because the bank was not the target of the attack. The bank's loss comes from its liability as card issuer (card reissuance costs, reimbursing fraudulent transactions, fraud monitoring). The fact that the article describes hacking techniques does not make it CYBER for the bank; what matters is WHO was attacked (the merchant/processor) and WHY the bank loses money (card issuer liability). Key characteristic: Perpetrator is external to the bank, though may manipulate bank employees into unknowingly facilitating fraud. Distinguisher: If bank employee intentionally participates or acts alone, classify as INTERNAL_FRAUD. If THE BANK ITSELF is directly hacked/attacked, classify as CYBER. If a THIRD PARTY is hacked and the bank bears costs as card issuer, classify as EXTERNAL_FRAUD (028_MCC). If employee is deceived but fraud originates externally, classify as EXTERNAL_FRAUD.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Large-scale compromise of card data through a breach at a THIRD-PARTY merchant or payment processor (NOT at the bank itself). The card-issuing bank bears financial losses (card reissuance, fraud reimbursement, monitoring costs) proportional to its share of compromised cards. This is EXTERNAL_FRAUD because the bank is a victim of external fraud — it was not attacked directly.
keySignals: third-party data breach affecting card issuer
scenarios.typicalExample: Target's payment systems are hacked, exposing 40 million credit card numbers. Banks that issued those cards collectively bear $200m+ in fraud losses and card reissuance costs. Each bank's loss is proportional to its share of compromised cards. The article describes the hack at Target, but for the issuing banks this is External Fraud — they were not attacked; they bear costs as card issuers.

contrastiveExamples.example: JPMorgan's OWN systems are hacked, exposing data of 76 million households. Here the BANK is the direct target → CYBER.
contrastiveExamples.distinction: In 028_MCC, a THIRD PARTY (merchant/processor) is hacked. In 012_CDC, the BANK ITSELF is hacked.

contrastiveExamples.example: Hackers penetrate bank's SWIFT terminal and steal $81m. The BANK's own systems are compromised → CYBER.
contrastiveExamples.distinction: In 028_MCC, funds are lost through card fraud liability, not through a direct attack on bank systems.

exclusions: If THE BANK'S OWN systems are hacked -> 012_CDC or 014_CFM (CYBER category)
If bank employee involved in stealing card data -> 030_ICC (INTERNAL_FRAUD)
If customer data (not cards) stolen from the bank -> 012_CDC (CYBER)

NEVER classify as CYBER just because the article mentions hacking — check WHO was hacked: if a merchant/retailer/processor was hacked and the bank bears costs as card issuer, it is 028_MCC (EXTERNAL_FRAUD)
scenarios.classificationPrinciple: Ask: Was THE BANK directly attacked? If NO (a third party was attacked and the bank bears cost as card issuer) → EXTERNAL_FRAUD (028_MCC). If YES (the bank's own systems were penetrated) → CYBER.
scenarios.note: Bank liability proportional to market share of issued cards. Even though the underlying event involves hacking, the bank's operational risk is External Fraud because it was not the target of the attack.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Fraudulent high-value transfer orchestrated by external fraudster with unwitting employee involvement
scenarios.typicalExample: External fraudster uses CEO impersonation to trick employee into wiring $50m to foreign account
exclusions: If cyber attack without human interaction -> 014_CFM

categories.categoryDescription: Intentional fraud, theft, or unauthorized activities by bank employees or insiders for personal gain: rogue trading (unauthorized positions hidden from management), employee embezzlement, internal theft of customer funds, theft and sale of card/customer data by staff, intentional fraudulent wire transfers by employees. Key characteristic: Employee acts intentionally to defraud or steal, concealing activities from management. Distinguisher: If employee misconduct is part of systematic business practice that harms customers and triggers regulatory fines (e.g., unauthorized account opening to meet sales targets), classify as CONDUCT, not INTERNAL_FRAUD. INTERNAL_FRAUD is for individual employee theft/fraud for personal benefit.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Bank employee steals or sells card data for fraudulent use
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank employee steals 100,000 card numbers and sells to criminals, causing $30m fraud losses
exclusions: If customer data (not cards) -> Could be internal fraud but not this specific scenario

scenarios.shortDefinition: Trader intentionally builds unauthorized position to speculate, resulting in large losses
scenarios.typicalExample: Trader hides $6bn unauthorized position over 2 years, causing $4.9bn loss when exposed

scenarios.shortDefinition: Employee intentionally executes fraudulent wire transfer for personal gain
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank employee diverts $20m in customer payments to personal accounts over 3 years
exclusions: If external fraudster with unwitting employee -> 029_WTX

categories.categoryDescription: Civil legal disputes and liability claims not arising from conduct violations or regulatory enforcement: employment class actions (discrimination, wage violations, wrongful termination, ERISA/retirement plan disputes), intellectual property infringement (patent, copyright, trademark disputes), contract disputes with non-regulatory parties, and employment-related lawsuits. Focus: Legal liability from civil law, not financial regulation. IMPORTANT — EMPLOYMENT LAWSUITS: All employment-related class actions (gender/racial discrimination, wage & hour violations, wrongful termination, ERISA/401k disputes) belong here in LEGAL, NOT in CONDUCT. Even if a regulator like the EEOC is involved, employment disputes are LEGAL because the plaintiff is an employee (not a customer or market participant) and the claim arises from labor/employment law (not financial regulation). CONDUCT is exclusively about financial misconduct harming customers, markets, or financial regulatory compliance. Distinguisher: If dispute involves financial regulatory enforcement (SEC, FCA, FINMA, etc.), customer mistreatment, or financial market misconduct, classify as CONDUCT. LEGAL is reserved for non-regulatory civil disputes, particularly employment and IP matters.

scenarios.shortDefinition: Bank found liable for violating third-party intellectual property rights (patents, trademarks, copyright)
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank pays $150m settlement for using patented financial technology without license
exclusions: If conduct-related dispute -> Conduct Risk category

scenarios.shortDefinition: Class action or large-scale lawsuit by employees against the bank for employment-related claims: discrimination (gender, race, age, disability), wage and hour violations (FLSA), wrongful termination, retirement plan disputes (ERISA/401k), and hostile work environment. This is LEGAL, NOT CONDUCT, because the plaintiffs are employees (not customers or market participants) and the claims arise from labor/employment law.
scenarios.typicalExample: Bank settles $250m gender discrimination class action with 3,000 female employees. Even though a government agency (EEOC) may be involved, this is LEGAL (034_WCA) because it is an employment dispute, not a financial regulatory action.

contrastiveExamples.example: Bank fined for mis-selling payment protection insurance to millions of retail customers → CONDUCT because the victims are CUSTOMERS and the violation is of financial consumer protection regulation.
contrastiveExamples.distinction: In 034_WCA, the plaintiffs are EMPLOYEES suing under labor/employment law. In CONDUCT, the victims are CUSTOMERS or MARKET PARTICIPANTS and the violation is of financial regulation.

exclusions: If the lawsuit is by CUSTOMERS (not employees) about financial products → CONDUCT (mis-selling, overcharging, etc.)
If financial regulatory enforcement (SEC, FCA, etc.) → CONDUCT
IMPORTANT: Employment lawsuits are ALWAYS LEGAL, even if a government agency like EEOC or DOL is involved, because these are labor law agencies, not financial regulators

classification_guidance.fundamental_principle: ALWAYS CLASSIFY BY ROOT CAUSE, NOT BY CONSEQUENCE. The presence of a regulatory fine, enforcement action, or lawsuit does NOT determine the category. What matters is the NATURE of the underlying event: Was it an intentional business practice violation (CONDUCT)? An unintentional error (ERROR)? A cyber attack on the bank (CYBER)? An external fraud scheme (EXTERNAL_FRAUD)? A non-malicious disruption (DISRUPTION)? An employment/IP civil dispute (LEGAL)?
general_rules: Always classify by ROOT CAUSE: What was the nature of the underlying event? Intentional misconduct → CONDUCT. Unintentional error → ERROR. Cyber attack on the bank → CYBER. External criminal scheme → EXTERNAL_FRAUD. Non-malicious disruption → DISRUPTION. Employment/IP dispute → LEGAL.
If news mentions multiple issues, classify based on the PRIMARY root cause event, not secondary consequences
A regulatory fine can result from ANY category (errors, cyber breaches, fraud, misconduct) — the fine itself does not determine the category
Ask WHO is the victim and WHAT LAW was violated to distinguish CONDUCT (financial regulation, customer harm) from LEGAL (employment law, IP law) from ERROR (unintentional process failure)

merchant_breach_is_external_fraud_not_cy.rule: When a MERCHANT, RETAILER, or PAYMENT PROCESSOR is hacked and card data is stolen, the card-issuing bank classifies this as EXTERNAL_FRAUD (028_MCC), NOT CYBER. The bank was NOT attacked — it bears costs as card issuer.
merchant_breach_is_external_fraud_not_cy.test: Was THE BANK's own system directly hacked? YES → CYBER. Was a THIRD PARTY hacked and the bank bears cost as card issuer? → EXTERNAL_FRAUD (028_MCC).
merchant_breach_is_external_fraud_not_cy.common_error: Articles about Target, Home Depot, Heartland breaches describe hacking in detail, which causes misclassification as CYBER. But the bank is the ISSUER bearing card replacement costs, not the ATTACK TARGET.

regulatory_fine_for_error_is_still_error.rule: When a bank is fined for an UNINTENTIONAL miscalculation, misreporting, or process failure (e.g., RWA errors, capital adequacy misstatements), classify as ERROR, NOT CONDUCT.
regulatory_fine_for_error_is_still_error.test: Was the underlying act INTENTIONAL misconduct (deliberate manipulation, willful violation)? → CONDUCT. Was it an UNINTENTIONAL error (calculation bug, methodology flaw, oversight)? → ERROR. The fact that a fine resulted does not change the root cause.
regulatory_fine_for_error_is_still_error.common_error: Articles about SEC/ECB fines for misreporting RWA mention 'regulatory fine' and 'enforcement action', which causes misclassification as CONDUCT. But the root cause is an unintentional model/calculation error → ERROR (037_RME).

employment_lawsuits_are_legal_not_conduc.rule: All employment-related disputes (discrimination, wage violations, wrongful termination, ERISA) are LEGAL (034_WCA), NOT CONDUCT, even if a government agency (EEOC, DOL) is involved.
employment_lawsuits_are_legal_not_conduc.test: Are the PLAINTIFFS employees suing under labor/employment law? → LEGAL. Are the victims customers/market participants and is financial regulation violated? → CONDUCT.
employment_lawsuits_are_legal_not_conduc.common_error: Employment discrimination lawsuits mention 'class action' and 'settlement', which can trigger CONDUCT classification. But CONDUCT is about financial misconduct harming customers/markets, not employment disputes.

algorithm_malfunction_fine_is_error.rule: When a trading algorithm MALFUNCTIONS (unintended behavior) and causes market disruption leading to a fine, classify as ERROR (038_TAE), NOT CONDUCT.
algorithm_malfunction_fine_is_error.test: Did the algorithm MALFUNCTION (bug, unintended behavior)? → ERROR (038_TAE). Was the algorithm DELIBERATELY designed to manipulate (e.g., spoofing by design)? → CONDUCT (001_ATC).
algorithm_malfunction_fine_is_error.common_error: Articles mention 'market manipulation' or 'regulatory fine' when an algorithm goes haywire, but if the algorithm was not intentionally designed to manipulate, it's an ERROR.

mis_selling_trio.010_MSR: Retail products: loans, credit cards, PPI, mortgages to consumers
mis_selling_trio.011_MSW: Wholesale products: RMBS, derivatives, structured products to institutions
mis_selling_trio.008_FID: Asset management products: mutual funds, ETFs, investment funds

esg_quartet.041_BSM: Bank's own ESG disclosure violations (greenwashing own reports)
esg_quartet.042_CSM: Bank liable for client's ESG issues (financing harmful projects)
esg_quartet.043_ESA: Shareholder lawsuit after bank's ESG scandal drops stock price
esg_quartet.044_FSD: Fund's portfolio not aligned with stated ESG objectives

cyber_vs_external_fraud.cyber_scenarios: MUST involve intentional attack directly on THE BANK's own infrastructure by external threat actor
cyber_vs_external_fraud.external_fraud_028_MCC: Third-party merchant/processor is hacked, bank bears cost as card ISSUER — this is EXTERNAL_FRAUD, not CYBER
cyber_vs_external_fraud.key_question: Was the BANK the direct target of the attack? CYBER. Was a THIRD PARTY attacked and the bank bears cost as card issuer? EXTERNAL_FRAUD.

cyber_vs_disruption.cyber_scenarios: MUST involve intentional attack by external threat actor on the bank
cyber_vs_disruption.disruption_scenarios: Non-intentional IT failures, natural disasters, supplier issues — no attacker involved

error_vs_conduct.error_scenarios: Root cause is UNINTENTIONAL: miscalculation, process failure, model bug, algorithm malfunction. May still result in regulatory fines.
error_vs_conduct.conduct_scenarios: Root cause is INTENTIONAL: deliberate market manipulation, willful regulatory violation, systematic customer abuse, knowing misrepresentation.
error_vs_conduct.key_question: Was the underlying act intentional misconduct or an honest mistake? Classify by intent, not by whether a fine resulted.

conduct_vs_legal.conduct: Victims are CUSTOMERS or MARKET PARTICIPANTS, violation is of FINANCIAL regulation (SEC, FCA, FINMA, etc.)
conduct_vs_legal.legal: Plaintiffs are EMPLOYEES, claims arise from LABOR/EMPLOYMENT law (discrimination, wages, ERISA). Also IP disputes.
conduct_vs_legal.key_question: Who is the plaintiff/victim? Customers/markets → CONDUCT. Employees → LEGAL. Third-party IP holder → LEGAL.

fraud_classification.external_fraud: Perpetrator is external to bank (may involve unwitting employee). Includes merchant breaches where bank is card issuer.
fraud_classification.internal_fraud: Perpetrator is bank employee acting intentionally for personal gain (not as part of systematic business practice)

confidence_scoring.0.9-1.0: Multiple key signals present, scenario explicitly mentioned in news
confidence_scoring.0.7-0.89: Several key signals present, clear match but not explicitly stated
confidence_scoring.0.5-0.69: Some key signals present, reasonable inference required
confidence_scoring.below 0.5: Weak match, consider NO_MATCH if confidence too low
